channel.title: Town on SuperList.us
channel.description: Recent content in Town on SuperList.us

item.title: Town of Dinosaur, Colorado
item.description: Originally called Artesia, this Colorado town renamed itself in 1966 to capitalize on its proximity to Dinosaur National Monument. They went all in — streets were renamed to things like Tyrannosaurus Trail, Brontosaurus Boulevard, and Antrodemus Alley. The commitment to the bit is honestly inspiring.

item.title: Accident, Maryland
item.description: Accident, Maryland, is a scenic small town in Garrett County. The name reportedly comes from a surveying mishap — two different parties accidentally claimed the same plot of land. The town leaned into it. It’s actually a beautiful mountain community, but good luck telling anyone where you live without them asking if you’re okay.

item.title: Hot Coffee, Mississippi
item.description: Hot Coffee, Mississippi, got its name from a traveler’s inn that served, you guessed it, really good hot coffee. A man named Davis brewed it with pure spring water and New Orleans beans, using molasses as a sweetener. People loved it so much they named the whole town after the drink. There is not currently a Starbucks.

item.title: Bat Cave, North Carolina
item.description: Bat Cave, North Carolina, is a real unincorporated community in Henderson County. Named after an actual bat cave in the nearby mountains. The mailing address is the best part — imagine putting “Bat Cave, NC” as your return address on every piece of mail you send. Bruce Wayne could never.

item.title: Truth or Consequences, New Mexico
item.description: In 1950, the host of the NBC game show “Truth or Consequences” promised to air an episode from the first town that renamed itself after the show. Hot Springs, New Mexico, took the deal. Over 70 years later, the name stuck and nobody changed it back. Locals call it “T or C” and the hot springs are still great.

item.title: Town of Chicken, Alaska
item.description: Chicken, Alaska is a surviving gold rush town with a summer population that occasionally cracks double digits. The story goes that residents wanted to name it “Ptarmigan” after the local bird, but nobody could agree on the spelling, so they just called it Chicken. There’s a gift shop, a café, and an amount of gold panning that is deeply optimistic.

item.title: Ding Dong, Texas
item.description: A small community in Bell County, Texas. Two men named Bell opened a store, hung a sign with two bells and the words “Ding Dong” on it, and that was that. The town has been Ding Dong ever since. There’s no doorbell joke that hasn’t already been made.

item.title: Why, Arizona
item.description: Why, Arizona exists because the state requires town names to have at least three letters. The intersection of Routes 85 and 86 formed a “Y,” but a single letter wouldn’t do. So they named it “Why.” Population: about 116. The most frequently asked question when you tell someone where you live is, appropriately, “why?”
